walkthrough.title: Open the Scenario Book
walkthrough.text: Let's get Gloomhaven: Jaws of the Lion set up. First, grab the spiral-bound scenario book and turn to the scenario you are about to play. The map is printed directly in the book, so there are no separate map tiles to assemble. Lay the book open flat on the table where everyone can see it.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Characters
walkthrough.text: Each player picks one of the four characters. The Demolitionist is a melee powerhouse who smashes obstacles and deals area damage. The Hatchet is a ranged attacker with a signature throwing axe. The Red Guard is a sturdy tank with shield abilities and fire attacks. The Voidwarden is a support class with mind control and ally buffs. Take the matching miniature, character board, ability cards, and attack modifier deck.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Character Boards
walkthrough.text: Set your character board in front of you. Dial your hit points to the maximum for your current level. Place your character sheet nearby for tracking experience, gold, and perks. If this is your first scenario, everything starts at level one with no perks applied.

walkthrough.title: Select Your Ability Cards
walkthrough.text: Each character has a hand of ability cards, usually nine to eleven at the start. Look through your cards and select your full starting hand. For the first scenario, just take all your available cards. Later in the campaign, you may have unlocked new cards and will choose a subset to bring into each scenario.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle Attack Modifier Decks
walkthrough.text: Let's prepare the attack modifier decks. Each player has a personal twenty card modifier deck. Shuffle yours and place it face down near your character board. Also shuffle the separate monster attack modifier deck and place it where everyone can reach it. These decks add randomness to every attack in the game.

walkthrough.title: Equip Your Items
walkthrough.text: If you have purchased any item cards between scenarios, slot them into your equipment slots now. Characters have slots for head, body, legs, two hands, and small items. For the very first scenario, you will not have any items yet, so you can skip this step.

walkthrough.title: Place Overlay Tiles
walkthrough.text: Now look at the scenario description in the book. It will tell you which overlay tiles to place on the map. These include obstacles like boulders and furniture, traps that deal damage when stepped on, hazardous terrain, and treasure tokens. Place each one on the hex indicated in the scenario instructions.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Monsters
walkthrough.text: The scenario tells you which monster types appear and where to place their standees. Place the cardboard monster standees on the hexes shown in the book. The scenario also shows different monster counts based on player count, usually with letters indicating which standees appear at two, three, or four players.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Monster Stat Sleeves
walkthrough.text: For each monster type in the scenario, find the matching monster stat sleeve. Slide the stat card to show the correct scenario level. The level determines monster hit points, attack values, and special abilities. For your first game, this will be level zero or one.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle Monster Ability Decks
walkthrough.text: Each monster type has its own ability card deck that controls its behavior. Find the ability deck for every monster type in the scenario. Shuffle each deck separately and place them face down near the corresponding stat sleeves. These cards are the monster artificial intelligence.

walkthrough.title: Gather Condition and Damage Tokens
walkthrough.text: Place all the condition tokens within reach of everyone. You will need poison, wound, immobilize, stun, muddle, strengthen, and invisible tokens throughout the scenario. Also gather the damage tracking tokens for marking monster hit point loss.

walkthrough.title: Draw Battle Goals
walkthrough.text: Each player draws two battle goal cards and secretly chooses one to keep. These are personal mini-objectives that reward bonus experience if completed during the scenario. Return the unchosen card to the deck without showing anyone.

walkthrough.title: Place Character Miniatures
walkthrough.text: Finally, place your character miniatures on the starting hexes shown in the scenario book. The starting hexes are usually grouped together near the entrance of the map. Players can choose which starting hex each character occupies.

walkthrough.title: Read the Scenario Introduction
walkthrough.text: Before round one begins, read the scenario introduction text aloud. This sets the narrative scene and sometimes reveals special scenario rules. Once everyone has heard the story, you are ready to begin. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Round Overview
walkthrough.text: Each scenario in Jaws of the Lion is played over multiple rounds. A round has three phases. First, every player secretly selects two ability cards from their hand. Second, everyone reveals their leading card to determine initiative order. Third, all figures take turns in initiative order, with players and monsters interleaved. Let's walk through each phase.

walkthrough.title: Card Selection Phase
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, you secretly pick two ability cards from your hand. One card is your leading card and its initiative number determines when you act. Lower initiative means you go earlier. You do not have to decide which card is top and which is bottom yet. You can also choose to declare a long rest instead of selecting two cards.

walkthrough.title: Determining Initiative
walkthrough.text: Everyone reveals their leading card at the same time. Then flip the top card of each monster type's ability deck. Now sort all figures by initiative number from lowest to highest. That is the turn order for this round. Ties between players and monsters go to the players first.

walkthrough.title: Playing Your Two Cards
walkthrough.text: When it is your turn, you play the top half of one card and the bottom half of the other. Top halves are usually attacks and special abilities. Bottom halves are usually movement and utility. You can pick which card provides the top and which provides the bottom. If neither half is useful, you can use any top half as a basic Attack two or any bottom half as a basic Move two.

walkthrough.title: Attack Actions
walkthrough.text: When you attack, you deal damage equal to the attack value on your card. Then you draw one card from your attack modifier deck. The modifier adjusts your damage up or down. You might draw a plus one, minus one, double damage, or even a miss. Range is shown on the card. Melee attacks hit adjacent hexes and ranged attacks reach further.

walkthrough.title: Movement and Positioning
walkthrough.text: Move actions let you move up to the listed number of hexes. You cannot move through enemies or obstacles. Some cards grant Jump, which lets you move through occupied hexes and obstacles. Positioning is critical because melee characters need to be adjacent to enemies while ranged characters want to stay at a safe distance.

walkthrough.title: Loss Cards
walkthrough.text: Some ability cards are marked with a loss icon, which looks like an X in a circle. These cards have very powerful effects, but once played they are removed from the game for the rest of the scenario. You never get them back. This is the most important mechanic in the game because your hand of cards is also your health timer.

walkthrough.title: Card Attrition and Exhaustion
walkthrough.text: Here is the core tension of the game. Every round you play two cards. When you rest, you get your discard pile back but must permanently lose one card. Loss cards are removed immediately. Over time your hand shrinks and your options dwindle. When you cannot play two cards at the start of a round because your hand plus discard pile is too small, you are exhausted and out of the scenario.

walkthrough.title: Short Rest vs Long Rest
walkthrough.text: You have two ways to recover discarded cards. A short rest happens at the end of any round. Shuffle your discard pile and randomly lose one card, then take the rest back into your hand. A long rest takes your entire turn. You skip playing cards, choose which card to lose from your discard, recover the rest, heal two hit points, and refresh spent items. Long rests are slower but give you control over which card you lose.

walkthrough.title: Damage Prevention
walkthrough.text: When you take damage, you have a safety valve. You can lose one card from your hand or two cards from your discard pile to completely negate all damage from one source. This keeps you alive but accelerates your card attrition. Use this sparingly for big hits that would otherwise knock you out.

walkthrough.title: Monster Turns
walkthrough.text: Monsters are controlled by their flipped ability card, which shows what they do this round. Monsters follow strict focus rules to determine who they target. They always focus on the closest character they can attack, using specific tiebreakers if multiple characters are equidistant. Monsters move the minimum distance needed to reach their focus target and then perform the actions on their ability card.

walkthrough.title: Conditions
walkthrough.text: Various abilities apply condition tokens to figures. Poison makes a target take plus one damage from all sources. Wound deals one damage at the start of each turn. Stun causes a figure to lose their next turn entirely. Immobilize prevents movement. Muddle forces the target to draw two modifier cards and use the worse one. Strengthen lets the target draw two modifier cards and use the better one. Invisible prevents enemies from focusing on the figure.

walkthrough.title: Loot and Items
walkthrough.text: When a monster is killed, it may drop a loot token. Move onto a loot token's hex to pick it up and gain gold. Between scenarios, you spend gold in the shop to buy item cards. Items are equipped to your character slots and provide powerful effects. Items are spent or consumed when used. Spent items refresh on a long rest or at the end of a scenario.

walkthrough.title: Experience and Leveling
walkthrough.text: You gain experience points during scenarios from specific card abilities marked with an XP icon. You also earn bonus experience from completing your battle goal. Between scenarios, you spend experience to level up, which unlocks new, more powerful ability cards and grants perk checkmarks. Perks let you permanently improve your attack modifier deck by removing negative cards or adding powerful ones.

walkthrough.title: Scenario Objectives and Difficulty
walkthrough.text: Each scenario has a specific objective, usually defeating all monsters in the final room. All players must still be standing when the objective is met to win. If everyone is exhausted before completing the objective, the scenario is lost, but you can retry with no penalty. Difficulty scales based on average party level divided by two, rounded up. You can adjust difficulty up or down for a harder or easier challenge.

walkthrough.title: Campaign Progression
walkthrough.text: Jaws of the Lion has twenty-five scenarios in a branching campaign. The first five scenarios serve as a tutorial, introducing one or two new mechanics each time. Full rules are in play from scenario six onward. Between scenarios, you draw city event cards for narrative choices, buy items from the shop, and level up your characters. Completing scenarios unlocks new scenarios and story branches.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Losing a Scenario
walkthrough.text: You win a scenario by completing its objective while at least one player is not exhausted. After winning, tally your gold from loot tokens, experience from the scenario and battle goals, and any bonus rewards. You lose if all players become exhausted before the objective is complete. Failed scenarios can be replayed immediately with no lasting penalty. Characters keep any experience and gold earned even in a loss.

walkthrough.title: The Tutorial Scenarios
walkthrough.text: The game has a brilliant built-in tutorial. Scenario one teaches basic movement and attack. Scenario two introduces conditions and more complex abilities. Scenarios three through five layer in the remaining rules gradually. By scenario six, all mechanics are active. The tutorial scenarios are part of the campaign story, so you are not just learning rules, you are playing the game. This is one of the best onboarding experiences in board gaming.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Scenario
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Gloomhaven: Jaws of the Lion! We are going to walk through a practice round together using two characters. The Demolitionist will be our melee fighter and the Hatchet will be our ranged attacker. Even if you are playing with a different party composition, the principles here apply to everyone. Let's jump in.

walkthrough.title: Selecting Your Cards
walkthrough.text: Round one begins. Each player picks two ability cards from their hand. The Demolitionist looks through nine cards and selects two that offer good movement and a solid attack. The Hatchet picks two cards with a ranged attack and a useful bottom action. Remember, you want your leading card's initiative to match your plan. Go early if you need to strike first.

walkthrough.title: Revealing Initiative
walkthrough.text: Both players reveal their leading cards. The Demolitionist has initiative twenty-six and the Hatchet has initiative forty-two. Now flip the monster ability card for the enemy type in the room. Let's say the monsters got initiative thirty-five. The turn order is: Demolitionist first, then monsters, then Hatchet.

walkthrough.title: Demolitionist Acts First
walkthrough.text: The Demolitionist goes first. They use the bottom half of one card for Move three to get adjacent to an enemy. Then they use the top half of their other card for Attack three. They draw an attack modifier card from their personal deck and get a plus one. That is four total damage, enough to take out a basic enemy in one hit. Nice start!

walkthrough.title: Monsters Strike Back
walkthrough.text: Now the monsters act. Their ability card says Move two, Attack two. Each monster focuses on the closest character it can attack. One enemy moves adjacent to the Demolitionist and attacks. The monster draws from the monster modifier deck and gets plus zero. Two damage hits the Demolitionist. Track the damage on the character board's HP dial.

walkthrough.title: Hatchet Takes a Shot
walkthrough.text: The Hatchet acts last this round. They use their top card for a ranged Attack three, targeting a monster two hexes away. The modifier draw is plus zero, so three damage lands. Then they use the bottom half of their other card for Move two, stepping back to maintain distance. The Hatchet wants to stay far from enemies whenever possible.

walkthrough.title: End of Round
walkthrough.text: Both players have used their two cards and all monsters have acted. The round is over. Both played cards go to each player's discard pile, unless they were loss cards. Nobody needs to rest yet since this is only round one. Pick up your cards from the discard and let's do it again.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: Card Selection
walkthrough.text: Round two begins. This time the Demolitionist wants to finish off a wounded monster, so they pick a card with low initiative to act early. The Hatchet spots a tempting loss card with a massive attack. Let's see how this plays out.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Burning a Loss Card Early
walkthrough.text: The Hatchet plays that powerful loss card for a devastating Attack five with area effect. It wipes out two enemies in one blow. Feels amazing, right? But that card is now gone for the rest of the scenario. The Hatchet just shortened their stamina by one full round. In a long scenario, this can mean getting exhausted before the final room.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Conserve Loss Cards
walkthrough.text: The number one mistake in Jaws of the Lion is playing loss cards too early. Your hand is your clock. Every card you lose permanently brings you one step closer to exhaustion. Save loss cards for emergencies or the final push when you know the scenario is almost won. Using a loss card in round two of a twelve round scenario is almost always wrong.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring Initiative
walkthrough.text: In round three, the Demolitionist picks two cards for their effects without paying attention to initiative. They end up with initiative sixty-eight. A wounded monster acts first, moves adjacent, and lands a big hit. If the Demolitionist had picked a faster card, they could have killed that monster before it attacked.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Initiative Is a Weapon
walkthrough.text: Initiative order is not just a formality. Going early lets you kill enemies before they hit you. Going late lets you see where enemies move before you commit your position. Always consider initiative when selecting cards. A slightly weaker attack with initiative fifteen is often better than a stronger attack at initiative sixty.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Forgetting Damage Prevention
walkthrough.text: The Demolitionist takes a massive six damage hit from a boss enemy. Their HP drops dangerously low. But wait. They forgot they could have discarded one card from their hand to negate all that damage completely. That option is always available and can save your character from a knockout.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Use Your Safety Net
walkthrough.text: Remember the damage prevention rule. Lose one card from your hand or two cards from your discard to negate all damage from one source. Yes, it costs stamina, but getting knocked out costs you the entire scenario. Use this against big hits from bosses and traps. Skip it for small pokes.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Scenario Rest
walkthrough.text: After several rounds, the Demolitionist is running low on cards. They declare a short rest at the end of the round. Shuffle the discard pile, randomly lose one card, and recover the rest. The Demolitionist is back in business with a refreshed hand, minus one card permanently.

walkthrough.title: Focus Fire Strategy
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about a key team strategy. Both characters should focus their attacks on the same enemy rather than spreading damage. A dead monster deals zero damage. If the Demolitionist hits one enemy for three and the Hatchet hits a different enemy for three, both monsters survive and attack next round. If they both hit the same target, it dies immediately.

walkthrough.title: Using Conditions Effectively
walkthrough.text: Now let's use some conditions. The Voidwarden in a full party can apply stun to a dangerous enemy, making it skip its next turn entirely. The Red Guard can immobilize ranged enemies, keeping them from getting into position. Conditions are force multipliers. Use stun on the biggest threat and poison on enemies that will survive multiple rounds.

walkthrough.title: Approaching the Final Room
walkthrough.text: As you clear enemies and progress through the scenario, you will eventually reach the final room with the objective. Before entering, check your hand size. If you only have three or four cards left, be very conservative. Use default actions and save your powerful cards for the final push.

walkthrough.title: Completing the Objective
walkthrough.text: The scenario objective is to kill all enemies in the final room. The Demolitionist and Hatchet coordinate their attacks. The Demolitionist goes early to tank damage and the Hatchet picks off weakened enemies from range. After clearing the last monster, the scenario is won! Collect your loot tokens, tally experience, and check your battle goals.

walkthrough.title: Post-Scenario Rewards
walkthrough.text: After winning, add up your gold from loot tokens and your experience from card abilities and battle goals. Check if you have enough experience to level up. Visit the item shop to spend gold on new equipment. Then look at the scenario book to see which scenario unlocks next in the campaign.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have played through your first practice scenario. You have seen how card selection, initiative, and the loss card system create deep tactical decisions every round. Remember the three key lessons. Do not burn loss cards early. Always consider initiative when choosing cards. And use the damage prevention rule to survive big hits.

walkthrough.title: Before You Enter the Dungeon
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into the full campaign, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategic fundamentals and common pitfalls.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to conditions, card types, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Stamina Management Is Everything
walkthrough.text: The single most important skill in Jaws of the Lion is managing your card stamina. Your hand of cards is your life timer. Every loss card played and every rest taken permanently removes one card. New players often play exciting loss cards in the first few rounds and then get exhausted before the scenario ends. Treat your loss cards like emergency supplies. Play non-loss cards for as long as possible and save the big plays for when they truly matter.

walkthrough.title: Focus Fire to Reduce Damage
walkthrough.text: A dead monster deals zero damage. This might seem obvious, but new players constantly spread their attacks across multiple enemies instead of focusing. If there are three monsters with four hit points each, do not deal four damage to three different targets. Kill one, then the next, then the last. Every enemy you eliminate before it acts is an attack you never have to absorb. Coordinate with your teammates and call out targets.

walkthrough.title: Master Initiative Timing
walkthrough.text: Initiative is one of your most powerful tools. Going early lets you kill enemies before they attack, which is crucial when a wounded monster could deal lethal damage. Going late lets you see where enemies move, which helps you position perfectly and avoid wasting movement. As a general rule, go early when you need to kill a dangerous threat and go late when you want to set up for next round. Always check your initiative number before committing to your card pair.

walkthrough.title: Understand Monster Focus Rules
walkthrough.text: Monsters are predictable once you learn the focus rules. They always target the closest character they can attack. If multiple characters are equidistant, they follow specific tiebreakers. Learning to predict where monsters will move and who they will attack lets you bait them into favorable positions. Use the Red Guard or Demolitionist as a tank by keeping them closest to dangerous enemies while the Hatchet and Voidwarden stay at range.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Rest Timing
walkthrough.text: Do not wait until your hand is empty to rest. Ideally, rest when you have used about half your cards. This keeps your hand options healthy while recovering discards. Short rests are quick but random. Long rests let you choose which card to lose, heal two hit points, and refresh spent items. If you have a specific card you must not lose, prefer long rests. If speed matters more, use short rests and accept the randomness.

walkthrough.title: Use Default Actions Liberally
walkthrough.text: Remember that any top half can be used as a basic Attack two and any bottom half can be used as a basic Move two. New players forget this and sometimes skip useful positioning because they think they must use the printed action on their cards. Default actions keep your cards in your discard pile instead of being lost, which preserves your stamina. When in doubt, default.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Neglect Battle Goals
walkthrough.text: Battle goals grant perk checkmarks when completed. Perks permanently improve your attack modifier deck, which makes every attack better for the rest of the campaign. A few extra plus one cards or removing negative cards compounds over many scenarios. Always check your battle goal at the start and keep it in mind throughout. The bonus experience also adds up for leveling.

walkthrough.title: Loot Efficiently
walkthrough.text: Gold buys powerful items that make your character significantly stronger. But chasing loot tokens in dangerous situations wastes movement and can get you killed. The best time to grab loot is after an area is clear of enemies or when a loot token is on your natural movement path. Do not cross the entire room to pick up a coin while monsters are still swinging at your teammates.

summary.title: Stamina Management Is Everything
bullets: Hand of cards = your life timer; every lost card brings exhaustion closer
Save loss cards for emergencies or final push
Play non-loss cards as long as possible

summary.title: Focus Fire to Reduce Damage
bullets: Dead monsters deal 0 damage — kill one at a time
Coordinate targets with teammates
Spreading damage leaves all enemies alive and attacking

summary.title: Master Initiative Timing
bullets: Go early to kill before enemies act
Go late to see enemy movement before committing
Always check initiative number on your leading card

summary.title: Understand Monster Focus Rules
bullets: Monsters target closest attackable character with tiebreakers
Predict movement to bait enemies into favorable positions
Tank (Red Guard/Demolitionist) stays closest; ranged stays back

summary.title: Plan Your Rest Timing
bullets: Rest when ~50% of hand is spent, not when empty
Short rest: fast but random card loss
Long rest: choose card, heal 2 HP, refresh items — but costs your turn

summary.title: Use Default Actions Liberally
bullets: Any top half = Attack 2; any bottom half = Move 2
Default actions keep cards in discard (not lost)
Preserves stamina when printed actions are not ideal

summary.title: Do Not Neglect Battle Goals
bullets: Perk checkmarks permanently improve your modifier deck
Removing negatives + adding positives compounds over campaign
Check goal at start, keep it in mind throughout

summary.title: Loot Efficiently
bullets: Gold buys powerful items for lasting character upgrades
Grab loot on natural movement path or after clearing enemies
Don't waste turns chasing coins while teammates take hits

walkthrough.title: Tempo Control Through Initiative
walkthrough.text: Advanced players manipulate initiative like a weapon. Pick two cards that have drastically different initiative values. This gives you the flexibility to choose fast or slow depending on the situation. If you need to kill a monster before it acts, lead with your low initiative card. If you want to see where enemies go first, lead with the high initiative card. The ability to shift tempo round by round is what separates good players from great ones.

walkthrough.title: Planned Loss Card Windows
walkthrough.text: Instead of avoiding loss cards entirely, plan specific windows to use them. Calculate how many rounds you can sustain based on your hand size and rest schedule. If the scenario will take twelve rounds and you have ten cards, you can afford to burn one or two loss cards at critical moments. The key is knowing your math. Count your rounds of stamina before the scenario starts and budget your loss cards accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Modifier Deck Optimization
walkthrough.text: Your attack modifier deck is the only permanent progression that carries between scenarios. Prioritize perk upgrades that remove negative modifiers first, especially the minus two card. Then add powerful positive cards like plus two or rolling modifiers. A clean modifier deck means more consistent damage, which means fewer wasted attacks and faster enemy kills. This compounds across the entire campaign.

walkthrough.title: Positional Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Advanced play uses monster focus rules offensively. By carefully positioning your characters, you can funnel monsters into kill zones. Place the tank two hexes from a doorway while ranged characters stand three hexes back. Monsters will move to the tank first, grouping up for area attacks. You can also use obstacles and doorway bottlenecks to prevent monsters from reaching your squishier characters.

walkthrough.title: Condition Stacking
walkthrough.text: Conditions become devastating when stacked intentionally. Apply poison to a tough enemy, then every subsequent attack deals bonus damage. Add wound for passive damage each turn. Stun the most dangerous monster at the perfect moment to buy one free round. In a coordinated team, one player applies conditions while the others capitalize with raw damage. The Voidwarden excels at this support role.

walkthrough.title: Item Synergy and Equipment Strategy
walkthrough.text: Items are not just stat bonuses. They are tactical options that expand what you can do each scenario. Boots that grant Jump let the Demolitionist bypass obstacles to reach backline enemies. A healing potion can save a damage prevention card. Items that grant bonus attacks or shields synergize with specific ability cards. Think about your item loadout as part of your card selection strategy, not separate from it.

walkthrough.title: Difficulty Tuning
walkthrough.text: The recommended difficulty formula is average party level divided by two, rounded up. But you can adjust up or down by one or two levels. Playing one level above recommended is the sweet spot for experienced players because it pushes you to play optimally without making scenarios feel impossible. If a scenario seems too easy, bump the difficulty up. If you lose twice in a row, consider lowering it. The campaign is about the story, not suffering.

walkthrough.title: Character Synergy and Party Composition
walkthrough.text: Each character fills a different role. The Demolitionist and Red Guard control the front line. The Hatchet deals consistent ranged damage. The Voidwarden supports with conditions and healing. Advanced teams coordinate their card selections each round through limited communication. You cannot reveal specific card numbers, but you can say things like "I am going early" or "I will handle that enemy." This coordination multiplies your party's effectiveness.

summary.title: Tempo Control Through Initiative
bullets: Pick cards with varied initiative values for flexibility
Lead fast to kill before enemies; lead slow to react
Shifting tempo round-by-round separates good from great

summary.title: Planned Loss Card Windows
bullets: Calculate stamina: hand size + rests = available rounds
Budget 1-2 loss cards at critical scenario moments
Know your math before the scenario starts

summary.title: Modifier Deck Optimization
bullets: Remove -2 card first, then other negatives
Add +2 and rolling modifiers for consistent damage
Clean deck compounds across entire campaign

summary.title: Positional Manipulation
bullets: Funnel monsters into kill zones using tank positioning
Doorways and obstacles create natural bottlenecks
Exploit focus rules to group enemies for area attacks

summary.title: Condition Stacking
bullets: Poison + raw damage = amplified kills
Stun most dangerous enemy at critical moment
Voidwarden applies conditions; others capitalize with damage

summary.title: Item Synergy and Equipment Strategy
bullets: Items are tactical options, not just stat boosts
Match items to ability card combos (Jump boots + melee rush)
Healing potion can save a damage prevention card

summary.title: Difficulty Tuning
bullets: Recommended: avg party level / 2, rounded up
+1 level = sweet spot for experienced players
Lower if losing twice in a row; campaign is about the story

summary.title: Character Synergy and Party Composition
bullets: Demolitionist/Red Guard = frontline; Hatchet = ranged DPS; Voidwarden = support
Coordinate through limited communication: 'I'll go early' / 'I'll handle that one'
Team coordination multiplies party effectiveness

entries.definition: Cards held in a player's hand, each with a top half action and a bottom half action. Two cards are played per round. Cards are the core resource and stamina timer.
An ability card marked with an X icon. When played for its loss effect, it is permanently removed for the rest of the scenario. Provides powerful one-time effects at the cost of stamina.
Cards drawn from a 20-card deck during every attack to adjust damage. Modifiers range from miss to double damage. Improved via perk upgrades.
AI cards flipped each round for each monster type, determining monster actions and initiative. Monsters follow the card instructions exactly.
A number on each ability card determining turn order. Lower initiative acts first. Ties between players and monsters favor players. Critical for tactical planning.
When a player cannot play two cards at the start of a round because their hand plus discard pile is too small, they are exhausted and removed from the scenario.
Performed at end of round. Shuffle discard pile, randomly lose one card permanently, recover the rest to hand. Quick but uncontrolled.
Takes your entire turn. Choose one card from discard to lose permanently, recover the rest, heal two HP, and refresh spent items. Slow but controlled.
At any time when taking damage, lose one card from hand or two cards from discard to negate all damage from one source. Emergency survival tool.
Any top half of a card can be used as Attack 2. Any bottom half can be used as Move 2. Useful when printed actions are not ideal.
The monster targeting system. Monsters target the closest character they can attack, with specific tiebreakers for equidistant characters. Predictable once learned.
Determines monster difficulty. Calculated as average party level divided by two, rounded up. Adjustable up or down for harder or easier play.
Condition: target gains plus one to all damage received. Removed when the target is healed. Devastating when stacked with high-damage attacks.
Condition: target takes one damage at the start of each of its turns. Removed when the target is healed. Passive damage over time.
Condition: target loses its entire next turn. Removed after the skipped turn. The most powerful single-target condition in the game.
Condition: target cannot perform any movement. The target can still attack if adjacent to an enemy. Removed at end of the target's next turn.
Condition: target draws two attack modifier cards and uses the worse result. Removed at end of the target's next turn. Reduces enemy damage reliability.
Condition: target draws two attack modifier cards and uses the better result. Removed at end of the target's next turn. Increases your damage reliability.
Condition: target cannot be focused by enemies. Monsters will skip the invisible character when determining focus. Removed at end of the target's next turn.
Melee damage dealer specializing in obstacle destruction and area-of-effect attacks. High damage output but needs to be in the thick of combat.
Ranged attacker with a signature throwing axe that can be recovered. Consistent damage dealer who prefers to stay at range.
Tank and defender with shield abilities and fire-based attacks. Excels at absorbing damage and protecting teammates.
Support class with mind-control abilities and ally buffs. Applies conditions, heals teammates, and manipulates enemy actions.
Spiral-bound book containing all scenario maps printed directly on the pages. No separate map tiles needed. Includes scenario objectives, narrative text, and monster placements.
Cardboard pieces placed on the map for obstacles, traps, hazardous terrain, and treasure. Placed according to each scenario's instructions.
Adjustable sleeve showing monster hit points, attack, movement, and special abilities at each scenario level. Slide to match current difficulty.
Movement type that allows passing through occupied hexes and obstacles. The destination hex must still be unoccupied. Granted by certain ability cards and items.
Movement type that ignores all terrain and figures. The destination hex must still be valid. Rare ability available to certain characters.
Attacks that hit multiple hexes as shown on the card diagram. Usually available on loss cards or high-level ability cards. Devastating for clearing groups of enemies.
Token dropped by defeated monsters or placed by scenario setup. Move onto the hex to collect and gain gold. Gold spent between scenarios at the item shop.
Equipment purchased between scenarios with gold. Slots into character equipment slots. Items are spent (flipped) or consumed (returned) when used. Refreshed on long rest or end of scenario.
Permanent modifier deck improvements earned from leveling up and completing battle goals. Each perk lets you add, remove, or replace cards in your attack modifier deck.
A secret personal objective drawn at scenario start. Completing it grants bonus XP and perk checkmarks. Two are drawn, one is kept.
Narrative event cards drawn between scenarios. Present a choice with mechanical consequences that can grant or cost gold, items, reputation, or other effects.
The win condition for each scenario, usually killing all monsters in the final room. Must be completed before all players are exhausted.
Gained during scenarios from card abilities marked with XP icons and from battle goal completion. Spent to level up between scenarios, unlocking new ability cards.
Overlay tile that triggers when a figure enters its hex. Deals damage or applies conditions. Destroyed after triggering. Can be disarmed by the Demolitionist.
Overlay tile that blocks movement and line of sight. Cannot be moved through except with Jump or Flying. The Demolitionist can destroy some obstacles.
Terrain that deals damage when a figure enters or begins its turn on that hex. Unlike traps, hazardous terrain is not destroyed after triggering.
